Q1: I tried to keep a consistent routine, usually studying in the afternoons after my morning classes. I'd block out about four hours most days, which adds up to my 28 hours weekly. Being a visual learner, I spent a lot of time making diagrams and color-coded notes from the materials. Sometimes my anxiety would mess with my focus, and I'd end up reorganizing my notes instead of actually learning new content, but at least I felt like I was doing something productive.
Q2: I aimed for regular times, but honestly, deadlines would throw everything off. When assignments were due, I'd cram way more hours into those days and then feel exhausted afterward. My anxiety makes me really stressed about missing deadlines, so I'd sometimes sacrifice sleep to make sure everything got submitted. The pattern was mostly consistent during calm weeks, but chaotic when things piled up.
Q3: Usually it was checking if new materials were posted or seeing if grades came back. I'd also log in when I remembered I had an assignment coming up and needed to double-check the requirements. Sometimes I'd just log in out of nervous habit, scrolling through to make sure I hadn't missed anything important. My anxiety definitely drove a lot of those check-ins.
Q4: Yeah, I got into a habit of logging in first thing in the morning and once before bed. It became almost automatic, like checking my phone for messages. The morning check was to see what I needed to focus on that day, and the night check was to calm my nerves about whether I'd missed something. It wasn't always productive, but it felt necessary for my peace of mind.
Q5: The video lectures were really helpful because I could pause and take visual notes, drawing out concepts as diagrams. I also found the assignment rubrics incredibly useful since they told me exactly what was expected. The discussion forums were okay too because seeing other students' questions sometimes clarified things I was confused about but too anxious to ask myself.
Q6: I often skipped the supplementary readings and some of the optional practice quizzes. With limited resources, I couldn't always print extra materials, and reading long texts on screen strains my eyes. I figured if it wasn't required and wouldn't directly affect my grade, I needed to prioritize the essentials. Time was tight, so I had to make choices about what was truly necessary.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my assignments or exams. Visual materials like infographics or video explanations were always worth it because they matched how I learn best. If something was going to help me complete my assignments well, I was motivated to engage with it fully, especially since my assignment scores were really important to me.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford. Required ones came with deadlines and consequences, so they automatically got priority in my schedule. With optional stuff, I'd think about doing it, but then my anxiety would kick in about the required work, and I'd skip the optional material. It's not that I didn't value them; I just couldn't justify the time when I was already stretched thin.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of lecture slides and PDFs early in the semester because my internet at home isn't always reliable. I'd study from printed notes and hand-drawn diagrams I made from the videos. I also used free YouTube tutorials when I needed concepts explained differently. A lot of my real studying happened with physical materials spread across my desk.
Q10: Most of my deep learning happened when I was working through problems on paper or redrawing diagrams from memory. I'd watch a video once online, then spend hours practicing concepts offline without logging back in. Group study sessions with classmates at the library also wouldn't show up anywhere in the LMS. My best understanding came from those quiet, offline moments.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download the lecture slides and maybe watch the video once to get the main ideas, then I'd work from my downloaded materials for days. My internet isn't great, so streaming videos multiple times wasn't practical. I'd take screenshots of important diagrams and study those on my phone during commutes or breaks between classes.
Q12: Midterms were brutal. I had five online courses all demanding attention at once, and my anxiety spiked really hard. There were days I'd sit at my desk for hours but couldn't focus because I was so overwhelmed thinking about everything I had to do. The mindfulness exercises they introduced helped a bit, but sometimes I still felt paralyzed by all the competing demands.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from staying up late on assignments, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely for a while. Financial stress from having limited resources also distracted me because I'd worry about things outside of school while trying to study.
Q14: There were plenty of times I'd have a video playing but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd be thinking about upcoming deadlines or worrying about my grades while the lecture played in the background. I'd technically be 'watching' the video, but I'd have to go back and rewatch it later because nothing actually stuck. My anxiety made it hard to be truly present sometimes.
Q15: I avoided discussion posts sometimes because participating made me anxious about saying something wrong in front of everyone. Even though I knew engagement mattered, I'd tell myself I'd do it later, then later never came. I also postponed starting difficult assignments because the thought of struggling with them stressed me out. Procrastination was definitely my way of managing overwhelming feelings.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my life. I'd check the calendar constantly and plan my week around what was due. My LMS activity would spike dramatically in the days before something was due. The anxiety of missing a deadline was so intense that I'd sometimes submit things early just to get the weight off my shoulders, even if I could have improved them with more time.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it motivated me to engage more with similar content. But if feedback was critical or confusing, I'd sometimes avoid that topic for a while because it made me anxious. Quick feedback helped me adjust my studying, while delayed feedback left me uncertain about whether I was on the right track. I really appreciated detailed comments on my work.
Q18: The weekly module structure helped me stay organized because I knew what to expect each week. Having clear deadlines for each module kept me on track, even if barely. But having five courses simultaneously made it hard to give each one proper attention. I appreciated when instructors posted everything at the start of the week so I could plan my limited time effectively.
Q19: Visual materials and clearly organized modules made engagement much easier for me. When content was text-heavy without diagrams or videos, I struggled to stay focused. Confusing navigation in some parts of the LMS frustrated me and wasted time I didn't have. The courses that had consistent formatting and visual aids were the ones I engaged with most naturally.
Q20: I think that could be anxiety-driven behavior, honestly. Someone might be logging in repeatedly to check things, watching videos without really absorbing them, or getting stuck in a loop of reviewing materials without feeling confident enough to submit work. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes it's just nervous energy that doesn't translate into completed assignments.
Q21: That actually describes me somewhat. My exam scores weren't great, but my assignment scores were really high because I put so much effort into them offline. I didn't need to be constantly logged in to do good work; I just needed the materials downloaded and quiet time to process them. Low LMS activity might just mean someone found efficient ways to learn outside the system.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and what you click on, but they can't show understanding or effort. They miss all the offline studying, the hand-drawn notes, the group discussions, and the mental energy spent worrying about the course. They also can't distinguish between mindless clicking and genuine engagement. It's a very incomplete picture of learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm genuinely curious about a concept and want to master it, not just pass the exam. For me, engagement looks like creating detailed visual notes, asking myself questions about the content, and connecting ideas across different topics. It's an internal state that doesn't always show externally.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my anxiety affected my engagement, or how the mindfulness intervention actually helped me cope better toward the end. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying from printed materials or the stress of managing five courses with limited resources. Most importantly, it wouldn't show that despite struggling with motivation and anxiety, I genuinely tried my best to learn and pass this course.
